sections.text: Welcome to Algebra One, Module One — variables and algebraic expressions. Algebra starts with one small trick: a letter standing in for a number we don't know yet. Master that move and the rest of algebra opens up.

Imagine boba tea is six dollars a cup. If three friends order, the bill is eighteen. If five order, it is thirty. Same rule, different number. We can write that one rule as six times x — and the letter does the changing for us.

Game plan. First, what a variable really is. Second, the vocabulary of an expression — terms, coefficients, constants. Third, how to evaluate when you know the number. Fourth, how to write expressions for real-life situations. By the end, you will write algebra the way you write a shopping list.

A variable is just a letter that stands for a number. Sometimes the number is unknown — we have not solved for it yet. Sometimes it changes — like the number of cups of boba. Either way, the letter holds a spot so the rule can stay the same while the number moves.

Look at three x plus seven. Three x is a term — three is the coefficient, x is the variable. Seven is a term too, but with no variable, so we call it a constant. The plus sign separates the terms. No equals sign anywhere — that is what makes this an expression, not an equation.

Evaluating means plugging a number in for the letter. Take three x plus five and let x equal two. Replace x with two — three times two plus five. Multiply first — six plus five. Add — eleven. Substitute, then simplify. Always in that order.

Two language traps cost students the most points here. Five less than x is x minus five — start with x, then subtract. A number tripled means three x — that is multiplication, not addition. Read the English carefully before you write the math, because the order really matters.
Your turn. Evaluate two a plus six when a equals five. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you are ready.
Time. Replace a with five — two times five plus six. Two times five is ten. Ten plus six is sixteen. The answer is sixteen. Substitute first, then simplify — same two-step rhythm every single time.

Writing real life as algebra. Six dollars per cup, x cups, becomes six x. Three more than n is n plus three. Twice your age, plus four, is two a plus four. The job is to translate English into letters and operations — that is what algebra is for.
Pause and try again. Write this as an algebraic expression: ten dollars less than three times the price p. Pause now. Write it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Three times the price is three p. Ten dollars less than that means subtract ten from three p — so the expression is three p minus ten. Order matters. Less than flips the subtraction, just like the trap we covered earlier.

Recap. A variable is a letter standing for a number. An expression mixes variables, numbers, and operations — and has no equals sign. Coefficient multiplies the variable, constant has no variable. To evaluate, substitute the value, then simplify.

This video is the overview. To actually master this module, do the rest. Read OpenStax Chapter one on the language of algebra. Run the Khan Academy practice set on writing algebraic expressions. Do the assignment in your Learn Portal dashboard — ten real-life situations as expressions. Next up, Module Two — Order of Operations.
